source.title: The Unengaged Mind: Defining Boredom in Terms of Attention

confidence_rationale: Foundational definitional paper that synthesises psychodynamic, existential, arousal, and cognitive theories of boredom into a single attention-anchored construct: 'the aversive experience of wanting, but being unable, to engage in satisfying activity.' Highest-relevance R1 paper for the functional-emotion-engagement-search account. Mapping to ARC-067 is direct: the architectural commitment that prolonged low-engagement is itself aversive maps onto Eastwood's 'wanting-to-engage-but-unable' definition. Source quality strong (peer-reviewed integrative review in Perspectives on Psychological Science); transfer risk modest because the construct is human-introspective and the REE substrate is computational, but the functional shape transfers cleanly.
failure_signatures: An REE substrate that does not produce action-pressure under sustained low-engagement (well-fed, safe, familiar, no z_goal) will fail to reproduce the Eastwood 'wanting-to-engage-but-unable' phenomenology and thus will not exhibit the boredom-driven exploratory behaviour Eastwood treats as definitional.

If the ARC-067 mechanism is implemented as inverted novelty (novelty-bonus going negative when novelty rate is low), the substrate will incorrectly mark genuinely novel-but-non-interactive territory as engaging, which Eastwood's definition rules out -- the agent is bored if it wants engagement but cannot achieve it, regardless of stimulus novelty.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Boredom is the aversive experience of wanting, but being unable, to engage in satisfying activity. It is fundamentally a state of unsuccessful attention engagement -- the bored individual experiences attention as un-occupiable by available stimuli, perceives time as slowed, and feels the situation as the cause of their unpleasant state.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-067 instantiates the 'wanting-to-engage-but-unable' state as a slow valence accumulator over an engagement-rate scalar. Eastwood's three-part construct (subjective time slowing + attribution-to-environment + want-engagement-fail) maps onto: (1) slow integrator over an engagement-rate proxy (commit transitions / residue-write rate / E3 deliberation depth) provides the time-slowing signal; (2) the aversive valence routes through z_harm_a (affective stream) so the agent treats the state as 'about something wrong' rather than internal-only; (3) the want-engage-fail loop is what closes when the engagement-rate-driven aversive recruits action-pressure on the same E3 score axis as harm. Eastwood's definitional anchor supports the R1 functional-emotion-engagement-search verdict.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Eastwood et al. is a theoretical synthesis, not a direct empirical paper -- the construct is built from introspective and behavioural literature. The 'wanting-but-unable' phrasing has computational ambiguity: 'wanting' could be agentive (predictive wanting in the MECH-216 sense, target-conditioned) or non-agentive (a pre-target activation pressure). ARC-067 takes the non-agentive reading; Eastwood does not arbitrate this directly. Cross-check with van Tilburg & Igou 2017 and Westgate & Wilson 2018 for finer-grained mechanism.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical synthesis paper in Perspectives on Psychological Science. Authors are York University and University of Guelph attention-and-mind-wandering researchers. The paper is the canonical citation for the modern functional definition of boredom and is cited in essentially every subsequent neuroscience and clinical paper on boredom.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high for a theoretical synthesis (peer-reviewed, top-tier psych venue, foundational status). Mapping fidelity high: the 'wanting-to-engage-but-unable' construct is the cleanest existing literature anchor for ARC-067's architectural shape. Transfer risk moderate because the construct is human-introspective and ARC-067 is computational; the functional shape transfers but the qualia framing does not.
